key_insight: When inverse PDE parameters are represented as grid-based fields, adjoint optimization is usually best, but when the unknown is naturally neural or functional, PINNs can be more efficient and effective.
engineering_relevance: For computational mechanics and solid mechanics inverse problems, method choice should be driven by how the unknown is parameterized, especially in time-dependent settings where adjoint storage and differentiation can become costly.
method: Fairly compare adjoint optimization and PINNs under the same domain, PDE, observations, regularization, optimizer, and precision.
Test on four benchmarks: unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability, 3D Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification.
Use a PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy to combine lower-cost initialization with adjoint-level accuracy.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports benchmark evaluations across four PDE-constrained inverse problems and concludes that performance depends strongly on the representation of the unknown, with PINNs achieving satisfactory reconstructions at lower cost in time-dependent cases.
watch_item: The comparison is sensitive to the chosen parameterization and implementation details, so results may not transfer directly if the unknown representation, optimizer, or precision changes.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
